quote: No. We're not looking to expand. We're doing a home renovation, so we need them removed and and then reinstalled until the home reno is finished.
stewart_read: This is the orphaned_flow trigger. Craig has existing solar and a non-new-solar need. The correct branch is the orphaned_flow protocol — probe ownership, system status, and then route to the appropriate service path. Bucky instead continues the standard verify flow, asking about title/co-owners as if Craig were a new-solar prospect. Reactive read holds: Bucky did not recognize the branch point.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky heard 'reinstalled' and interpreted it as a potential new-system opportunity, deliberately staying on the verify path to qualify Craig for a new install. Rejected — Craig's language ('not looking to expand,' 'until the home reno is finished') is unambiguous about the temporary, service-only nature of the need.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky the orphaned_flow section. When a customer discloses existing solar, the first question is ownership/status — not a continuation of the new-solar verify flow. The branch point is at 00:36 ('Do you already have solar?') and Bucky missed it. Manager should walk through what the correct branch looks like for a removal/reinstall request specifically.

quote: I'm not looking to buy any more panels. I just need the removal and reinstallation.
stewart_read: Craig's second explicit statement of his removal-only need, this time as a direct correction to Bucky's credit-score question. Bucky's response ('I'm just getting information together so we can do a design for your home') does not address the removal constraint and pivots to the bill-swap pitch. This is the second missed orphaned_flow branch — the correct move here was to directly address whether Ion can service a removal request, not to redirect to a new-solar design.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky recognized the mismatch but believed the bill-swap pitch could reframe Craig's need as a new-solar opportunity, deliberately deferring the routing question. Partially supported by the fact that Craig did re-engage at 02:53 — but the strategy was incomplete because Bucky never addressed the removal constraint even after re-engagement.
coaching_implication: The bill-swap pitch at 02:28 is not the error — Craig's re-engagement at 02:53 shows it had some effect. The error is that Bucky never paired the pitch with an answer to Craig's removal question. Manager should coach the sequence: acknowledge the removal need explicitly, then introduce the bill-swap as an additional option, then route to specialist for both. The removal question needed a direct answer before the pitch could land.

quote: Okay. Yeah. We we can continue then. I'm I'm curious to hear more.
stewart_read: This is the most important moment in the call and the one Stage 3 must not miss. Craig re-engages directly after the bill-swap pitch — the call has a genuine second chance. The correct move here is to acknowledge Craig's removal need explicitly ('I hear you on the removal — let me make sure we can handle that, and separately, here's what the specialist can show you on the bill side') and then route to the specialist for both threads. Instead, Bucky immediately asks for the power bill, treating Craig's re-engagement as permission to continue the new-solar path without addressing the removal constraint. The window closes at 03:09.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bucky heard 'I'm curious to hear more' as full buy-in and moved forward on the new-solar path. This is the more supported read — Bucky does not acknowledge the removal constraint at all after 02:53, suggesting he interpreted re-engagement as resolution of the mismatch.
coaching_implication: This is the call's teachable moment. Play the 02:53 re-engagement for Bucky and ask: 'What did Craig just give you, and what did you do with it?' The answer is that Craig gave Bucky a second chance and Bucky spent it on the power bill instead of addressing the removal question. The coaching move: when a customer re-engages after a mismatch, acknowledge the original concern before advancing the new thread.

quote: do you guys just have the service to remove the panels?
stewart_read: Craig asks a direct, answerable service question. Bucky's response — 'I know we can remove it. We just...' followed by a pivot back to the design path — is a framing error on the universal_escape_hatch. The escape hatch ('that's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') would have been the correct move here: acknowledge the question, confirm it can be answered, and route to the specialist. Instead, Bucky offers an unconfirmed 'I know we can remove it' and immediately walks it back into the design path, which Craig correctly reads as a non-answer.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky said 'I know we can remove it' as a genuine attempt to confirm the service and then route to the specialist for details. Rejected — the pivot back to 'build you the design we're doing now' undercuts the confirmation and signals Bucky does not actually know whether standalone removal is available.
coaching_implication: Manager needs to resolve the operational question first: does Ion offer standalone panel removal? If yes, Bucky needed a routing path ('let me connect you with our service team') and the universal_escape_hatch language. If no, Bucky needed an honest acknowledgment ('we don't offer standalone removal, but here's what we do offer'). Either way, 'I know we can remove it. We just...' is the wrong answer — it promises something Bucky cannot confirm and then retreats. Coach the escape hatch as the correct frame for any service question outside the setter's scope.

quote: We have a program. It's called the the bill swap. And, we actually remove your power bill, and we replace it with a lower and cheaper solar bill.
stewart_read: The bill-swap pitch is delivered after Craig has stated his removal need twice (00:55 and 02:04). On its face this looks like a complete misread of the customer. But Craig's re-engagement at 02:53 ('I'm curious to hear more') shows the pitch partially landed — Craig was not entirely closed to the new-solar conversation. The pitch itself is not the failure; the failure is that it was delivered without first addressing the removal constraint, so when Craig re-engaged, Bucky had no bridge between the removal need and the new-solar opportunity.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Bucky deployed the bill-swap pitch as a deflection from the removal question, hoping to redirect Craig onto the new-solar path entirely. Partially supported — but Craig's re-engagement suggests the pitch had genuine effect, making the strategic read (deliberate reframe attempt) more defensible than it first appears.
coaching_implication: The bill-swap pitch is not the problem to fix. The problem is the missing bridge: 'I hear you on the removal — let me make sure we get that handled, and while we're at it, here's something that might change the math on your bill.' That sequence acknowledges the removal need, keeps the door open for new solar, and gives Craig a reason to stay on the call. Coach Bucky on sequencing: address the stated need first, then introduce the additional opportunity.

quote: Is there any way on the title but you and your wife? Or sorry. Just you?
stewart_read: Bucky asks the title question correctly and Craig discloses that Mary Anne Pimodora owns the home. Bucky's follow-up — 'Is she looking into solar too?' — is a reasonable probe but misses the more important implication: Mary Anne is the homeowner, not Craig. On a new-solar call, this would be a critical qualification gap (the decision-maker is not on the call). On this call, the homeowner-vs-occupant distinction is less consequential because Craig's need is a service request, not a purchase decision. Flagged because the title question was asked but the ownership implication was not fully processed.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky noted Mary Anne as the homeowner and planned to include her in the appointment. Not supported — Bucky does not reference Mary Anne again after 01:13, and the call ends before any appointment is discussed.
coaching_implication: On any new-solar call, when the homeowner is someone other than the person on the phone, the correct move is to confirm the homeowner's availability for the appointment before advancing. On this call the point is moot given the service-request nature, but the pattern is worth flagging: Bucky asked the title question and then did not act on the answer.

quote: Man, I'm I'm sorry. I'm actually good. I'm I'm this isn't what I'm looking for. I appreciate your help.
stewart_read: Craig ends the call politely and without hostility. Bucky's response ('Okay. No problem at all. Okay. You have a great day.') is appropriate in tone but represents a missed final routing opportunity. If Ion offers standalone removal, the correct close here is a warm handoff: 'Before you go — let me get you the number for our service team, they handle exactly what you're describing.' If Ion does not offer it, a referral or honest acknowledgment would leave Craig with a better impression. The call ends with Craig's actual question unanswered.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky recognized the call was over and chose a clean exit over a forced routing attempt. Defensible — Craig's tone was final. But the routing offer costs nothing and could have recovered the interaction.
coaching_implication: Coach the exit move: even when a call is clearly ending, a warm routing offer ('let me get you to the right team') is low-cost and preserves the relationship. On a service-request call that was misrouted, the exit is the last chance to leave Craig with a positive Ion impression. Manager should also use this moment to confirm with Bucky whether he knew Ion's service team existed as a routing option.
